mechanism: ACE inhibitors combined with NSAIDs increase risk of acute kidney injury, hyperkalemia, and reduced antihypertensive effect.
recommendation: Avoid routine combination. Prefer acetaminophen for pain. If NSAID needed short-term, monitor BP, creatinine, potassium.

mechanism: NSAIDs increase bleeding risk via antiplatelet effect and potential GI erosion; some NSAIDs also displace warfarin from protein binding.
recommendation: Avoid if possible. Prefer acetaminophen. If unavoidable, use lowest dose + GI protection and monitor INR.

mechanism: Amiodarone inhibits CYP2C9 and P-glycoprotein, significantly increasing warfarin levels and bleeding risk.
recommendation: Reduce warfarin by 30-50% when starting amiodarone; recheck INR in 3-5 days.

mechanism: Risk of lactic acidosis if patient has pre-existing renal impairment receiving IV contrast.
recommendation: Hold metformin at time of contrast and for 48h; restart once renal function confirmed stable.

mechanism: Risk of fatal serotonin syndrome from combined serotonergic activity.
recommendation: Absolute contraindication. Allow 2-week washout (5-week for fluoxetine) between agents.

mechanism: Both are serotonergic; additive risk of serotonin syndrome and seizure.
recommendation: Use cautiously. Monitor for tremor, clonus, hyperreflexia, agitation. Consider alternative analgesic.

mechanism: Macrolide CYP3A4 inhibition raises statin (esp. simvastatin) levels → rhabdomyolysis risk.
recommendation: Hold simvastatin/lovastatin during macrolide course; prefer azithromycin if macrolide needed.

mechanism: Additive negative chronotropy/inotropy → bradycardia, heart block, decompensated HF.
recommendation: Avoid combination outside cardiology guidance. If needed, monitor HR and PR interval closely.

mechanism: Synergistic vasodilation → profound, potentially fatal hypotension.
recommendation: Absolute contraindication within 24h (48h for tadalafil). Alternative antianginals required.

mechanism: Increased incidence of drug rash when combined.
recommendation: Counsel patient; consider cephalosporin alternative if severe rash history.

mechanism: Additive CNS and respiratory depression — leading cause of overdose deaths.
recommendation: FDA black box warning. Avoid combination. If unavoidable, lowest doses, shortest duration, naloxone on hand.

mechanism: Binge alcohol increases risk of lactic acidosis with metformin.
recommendation: Counsel on moderation. Hold metformin if binge/hospitalized drinking.

mechanism: P-glycoprotein inhibition raises digoxin levels → toxicity (arrhythmia, GI, vision changes).
recommendation: Reduce digoxin dose by 50% and monitor levels.

mechanism: Reduced levothyroxine absorption when co-administered.
recommendation: Separate administration by at least 4 hours.

mechanism: Many antibiotics disturb gut flora and inhibit CYPs → INR increases.
recommendation: Recheck INR 3-5 days after starting antibiotic; adjust warfarin dose.

mechanism: Non-selective beta blockers (propranolol) blunt bronchodilation and may trigger bronchospasm.
recommendation: Prefer cardioselective β1 blockers (metoprolol) in asthma/COPD; monitor bronchospasm.

mechanism: Beta blockers mask adrenergic hypoglycemia symptoms (tremor, tachycardia).
recommendation: Educate patient to monitor glucose more closely; diaphoresis still present.

mechanism: Combined GI ulcer/bleed risk.
recommendation: Add PPI if combination needed >1 week.

mechanism: Additive QT prolongation → torsades risk.
recommendation: ECG if multiple QT agents; correct Mg/K; consider alternative antibiotic.

mechanism: Reduced clopidogrel activation via CYP2C19 inhibition (clinical significance debated).
recommendation: Prefer pantoprazole if PPI required; counsel on bleeding balance.
